Describes the evolution and improvements of the Pebble dbgserial console leading to PULSEv2.

# Purpose
The document provides a historical overview of the development and evolution of the Pebble dbgserial console, leading to the design of PULSEv2. Initially, the dbgserial port was used for debugging firmware by printing log messages and allowing interaction through a terminal emulator. Over time, the limitations of this setup became apparent, especially in automated integration testing, where the serial console proved unreliable. To address these issues, PULSEv1 was developed, introducing a link-layer protocol to improve flash imaging performance and provide integrity assurance. However, PULSEv1 had its own flaws, such as checksum vulnerabilities and lack of reliable transmission for certain applications. PULSEv2 was then designed to resolve these issues, offering reliable datagram service, improved synchronization, and future-proofing through an option-negotiation mechanism. This new protocol significantly enhanced test automation reliability and efficiency, becoming the standard for developers and the factory.
# Content Summary
The document provides a historical overview of the development and evolution of the Pebble dbgserial console, leading to the design and implementation of PULSEv2. Initially, the dbgserial port was used for logging and debugging firmware, with an interactive prompt for developers and manufacturing. However, the system was slow and cumbersome, and the introduction of automated integration testing exposed its unreliability, leading to numerous workarounds.

PULSEv1 was developed to address these issues, particularly focusing on improving the flash imaging process, which was a bottleneck in manufacturing. PULSEv1 introduced a link-layer protocol that allowed for best-effort multiprotocol datagram delivery with integrity assurance. This protocol significantly reduced imaging time and allowed for the implementation of other protocols, although it faced challenges with reliability and integrity due to flaws in its checksum and lack of explicit length fields.

The limitations of PULSEv1 prompted the development of PULSEv2, which aimed to resolve these issues and fully replace the legacy serial console. PULSEv2 was designed with test automation in mind, offering improved communication and synchronization, a standard checksum, and explicit packet lengths. It supports both best-effort and reliable datagram services, facilitating the implementation of prompt commands and other protocols. PULSEv2 was adopted for test automation in May 2016, leading to reduced test run times and improved reliability, and became the default for firmware developers by September 2016.
